parentQuestion: A patient awakens from surgery unable to form new memories. They can recall childhood events perfectly and hold conversations, but 5 minutes later, they don't remember the conversation occurred. Procedural learning (motor skills) remains intact. Which structure was most likely damaged during surgery?

scaffolds.q: Memory is not a single system - there are different types stored in different brain regions.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Declarative memory (facts, events) uses different structures than procedural memory (motor skills). They can be damaged separately.

scaffolds.q: The hippocampus is critical for forming new declarative memories (facts and events).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The hippocampus in the medial temporal lobe is the 'memory encoder' - it binds together new experiences into storable memories.

scaffolds.q: Inability to form NEW memories is called 'anterograde amnesia'.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Anterograde = going forward. The patient can't make new memories from this point on, though old memories may be preserved.

scaffolds.q: Old memories that were already stored before the injury can still be recalled.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Once memories are consolidated to the cortex, they don't need the hippocampus to be retrieved. That's why childhood memories are preserved.

scaffolds.q: Procedural memory (motor skills, habits) uses the cerebellum and basal nuclei, NOT the hippocampus.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! That's why this patient can still learn motor skills - the procedural memory system is intact. The hippocampal (declarative) system is damaged.

scaffolds.q: Working memory (holding a conversation) uses prefrontal cortex, not hippocampus.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The patient can hold conversations because working memory is intact. They just can't transfer that information to long-term storage.

scaffolds.q: Bilateral hippocampal damage causes severe anterograde amnesia (can't form new memories).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The famous patient H.M. had this exact pattern after bilateral hippocampal removal - trapped in an eternal present.

scaffolds.q: Old memories preserved + new memories impossible + procedural learning intact = hippocampal damage.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This dissociation pattern is the signature of hippocampal damage. Other memory systems remain functional.

scaffolds.q: The answer is HIPPOCAMPUS - the memory encoding structure for new declarative memories.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not the amygdala (that's for emotional memory). Not the prefrontal cortex (working memory is intact). Not the basal nuclei (procedural memory works). The hippocampus encodes new declarative memories.
